Title: Exercise as a Causal Modulator of Inflammation and Respiratory Health

Abstract:
Regular aerobic exercise causally reduces chronic inflammation through multiple
immunological pathways. This RCT (n = 312) demonstrates that a 12-week structured
exercise intervention significantly reduced inflammatory markers and improved
lung function in urban adults with moderate air pollution exposure.

Introduction:
While it is well established that inflammation causes respiratory disease, the role
of physical activity in modifying this causal pathway is underexplored. Exercise
is known to reduce baseline inflammatory tone, but whether exercise can interrupt
the pollution-inflammation-disease pathway has not been tested directly.

Methods:
Randomized controlled trial. Participants (non-smoking adults, age 30-65) in a
high-pollution urban area. Intervention: supervised aerobic exercise (150 min/week)
for 12 weeks. Control: usual activity. Outcome: IL-6, TNF-α, FEV1/FVC ratio.

Results:
Exercise intervention caused a 23% reduction in IL-6 levels (95% CI: 15-31%, p < 0.001)
compared to the control group. TNF-α was reduced by 18% (p = 0.004).

Importantly, exercise caused improved lung function (FEV1/FVC ratio +4.2%, p = 0.012)
independent of pollution exposure, suggesting exercise can partially counteract the
inflammation caused by air pollution.

The mechanism by which exercise reduces inflammation involves increased production of
anti-inflammatory myokines (IL-10, IL-15) during muscle contraction. These myokines
suppress NF-κB signaling, the same pathway activated by air pollution.

Exercise also causes improved mucociliary clearance, which reduces the residence time
of inhaled particulates in the airways and may directly reduce the inflammatory trigger.

Discussion:
This trial provides causal evidence that exercise modulates the pollution-inflammation
pathway. The interaction is mechanistically coherent: exercise causes IL-10 upregulation
which suppresses the same inflammatory signaling activated by PM2.5.

Whether the exercise-induced reduction in inflammation is sufficient to clinically
meaningful prevent respiratory disease in high-pollution areas remains an open question.
The dose-response relationship between exercise intensity and anti-inflammatory effect
is not fully characterized.

Conclusion:
Exercise causally reduces inflammation through myokine-mediated immunomodulation and
improved mucociliary clearance. Future studies should test whether exercise can meaningfully
modify the causal pathway from air pollution to respiratory disease outcomes.
